pi_items.source: Fail2Ban security configuration guides and examples (Multiple sources: Medium article on Fail2Ban SSH configuration, SecOps Solutions, ArchWiki, GitHub fail2ban/fail2ban repository). Standard practice shows maxretry = 3 as a strict security policy for SSH brute-force protection.

Fail2Ban configuration documentation (Linux Hint, OneUptime, ArchWiki). The nginx-limit-req jail example shows bantime = 7200 (2 hours). This is a valid security configuration representing 2 hours of ban duration, which falls between shorter 1-hour bans and longer 24-hour bans, following industry best practices.

Official Fail2Ban documentation (ArchWiki, RedHat blog, GitHub fail2ban/fail2ban repository, Linode docs). The standard jail name for SSH in Fail2Ban is '[sshd]' (not '[ssh]'). This is the correct configuration name used in /etc/fail2ban/jail.conf and all official documentation.

privileged_info_summary: The task configures Fail2Ban SSH protection with three verified configuration parameters: maxretry = 3 (strict security policy allowing 3 failed attempts before banning), bantime = 7200 (2-hour ban duration), and jail_name = 'sshd' (standard Fail2Ban jail name for SSH). All values are real, legitimate configuration values confirmed in official Fail2Ban documentation and widely-adopted security best practices. The setup script resets the service to default values (maxretry=5, bantime=600) to ensure the agent performs the actual configuration work.
